Their reaction is not this at all. Their reaction is to fold up. Because people can't stand this. They just can't stand it. You call somebody an asshole, they can't take it. They cannot deal with the idea of being walked around, walking around, and having some significant number of people thinking that they are a complete jackass. Trump is another great example. New Yorkers, more than anybody else, they can't stand them. I mean, just can't stand them. You would think if you didn't know, boy, you mentioned them in the home territory. That's, you know, nuh, nuh, nuh, nuh, nuh, nuh, nuh. If you saw that picture, we were in Atlantic City, and going on to speaking in the, my plane engine broke, and so we're waiting for another plane, and the Trump helicopter's sitting over there, so we all got pitched in. And we said something to the people inside the private air terminal, who you understand are the vendor to Trump as the client, right? And we said, you know, do you think anybody, we didn't see anybody around, and I didn't see anybody I knew, obviously, so I said, do you think they'll mind if we run out there and take a picture? And so the person at the counter, now keep in mind, Trump's a customer, right? The person says, what that asshole doesn't know won't hurt him. Now, he's paying their salaries, right? But welcome to what most of New York thinks about him. I don't, I doubt he gives it the first, but most people cannot, they cannot take this. And it really gets in their way a lot. So, for the sake of time, and because people moaned, I am going to squeeze in a mid-afternoon break, too. Jumping over some stuff and leaving it for you to read on your own. But the one thing I did want to make sure we did before we get out of here, and we're, you know, approaching the wire, I wanted to have you do what I call the exercise. And I should tell you ahead of time that when I do this with clients, and I don't often do it because it's painful, so I usually give it to them, tell them to go home and do it. And you'll see as you do it. And the exercise has to do with all resistance, external, internal, really many efforts. But it's really the most pregnant question I've ever been asked. And I was asked it for the first time, I don't know, 27, 28 years ago. And it's preceded sort of by a discussion of where do you want to be, not necessarily just financially, but for our purposes, that's it. I will usually in a consulting gig phrase it like, well, tell me what your business looks like now, and then tell me what you want it to look like, and I don't mean red brick, you know, but tell me what you want it to look like, I don't know, say three years from now, or five years from now, or seven years from now, or if you have a benchmark place in mind, tell me what you want it to look like then. So that's like the precursor to this. And so it might be what you're in come now, what you want your income to be, presumably, well some of you already seven figures and you're here more about slight edge and sustainability than you are anything else, but you saw the stats. Maybe in the room are not in seven figures, but probably came in part thinking you want to be in seven figures. So you can narrow it or broaden it, it really does make any difference. The exercise works no matter how narrow or how broad and whether you're dealing with personal stuff or business stuff that may be in difference. It's really human potential exercise, it's not a business exercise, but it works in business. And so I'll usually do it in a client setting with that precursor. Tell me what your business looks like now, and they'll tell me, you know, what the income is and what the customers are like and how much they work and where their customers come from. All the subset questions that like, you know, where do they come from and what do you do to get them. And then I'll ask more touchy-feely questions actually, well, do you like your customers? What do you like about your business? What do you don't like about your business? And then what do you want it to look like in three years or four years and five years, whatever that number is. And now having set up sort of the parameters for descriptions, so now that answer will now come.
